clinicalSummary.oneSentence: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is a chronic disorder of insulin resistance and progressive beta-cell dysfunction that raises glucose over time.
clinicalSummary.keyMechanism: Peripheral tissues become less responsive to insulin and pancreatic beta cells can no longer compensate fully.
clinicalSummary.timeScale: usually develops over years and is often discovered in ambulatory care
clinicalSummary.patientExperienceSummary: Some patients notice thirst, polyuria, and fatigue, while many are identified on routine lab work before dramatic symptoms appear.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: Readers need the normal insulin-glucose loop before the detectives can show how resistance breaks it.

pathophysiology.event: insulin resistance increases in muscle and liver
pathophysiology.mechanism: Tissues need more insulin to move glucose effectively, especially after meals.
Compensatory insulin production eventually fails to keep glucose in range.

physicalExamClues: evidence of metabolic risk rather than a single dramatic focal finding
presentation.typicalTimecourse: often gradual enough that outpatient screening and longitudinal follow-up matter.

labs.name: hemoglobin A1C
labs.expectedFinding: elevated A1C supports diagnosis in the appropriate setting

diagnosticLogic: Confirm chronic hyperglycemia with standard diagnostic thresholds rather than a single symptom story alone.
Risk profile and comorbid disease shape treatment urgency and medication selection.

differentials.whyLessLikely: the longitudinal ambulatory pattern and metabolic-risk context fit type 2 diabetes better

escalation: add glucose-lowering medication based on A1C, cardiovascular risk, kidney disease, and tolerance
notes: Outpatient diabetes care ties glucose control to prevention of organ complications.

evidence.claimText: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is driven by insulin resistance with progressive failure of beta-cell compensation.
Type 2 diabetes is commonly diagnosed through standard blood glucose and A1C testing rather than symptoms alone.
Management combines lifestyle change, glucose lowering therapy, and complication prevention based on comorbidity risk.

governanceNotes: Use for baseline mechanism, symptom, and diagnosis framing.
Keep only for audit history; current pack supersedes it.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Insulin resistance comes before obvious organ damage
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: Show the metabolic imbalance early so later complication-prevention choices feel earned.

visualAnchors.label: Clinic desk with glucometer, A1C trend, and prevention checklist
visualAnchors.whyItMatters: The visual language should feel longitudinal and primary-care oriented rather than emergency-only.

evidenceRelationships.notes: Medication choice and prevention strategy depend on comorbidity context, not glucose alone.

clinicalSummary.oneSentence: Essential hypertension is persistent elevated blood pressure without a single reversible cause and is often silent until it causes end-organ strain.
clinicalSummary.keyMechanism: Vascular tone, sodium handling, and long-term cardiovascular regulation shift blood pressure upward over time.
clinicalSummary.timeScale: usually chronic and ambulatory rather than dramatic at onset
clinicalSummary.patientExperienceSummary: Many patients feel fine, which makes screening, confirmation, and prevention central to the story.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: Readers need a baseline for how vascular resistance and volume affect pressure.

pathophysiology.event: vascular resistance and pressure stay chronically elevated
pathophysiology.mechanism: Long-term hemodynamic stress raises cardiovascular and renal risk even when symptoms are absent.

commonSymptoms: nonspecific headache when pressure is very high
commonSigns: persistently elevated office or home blood pressure
historyClues: sleep apnea or obesity risk factors
physicalExamClues: repeat measurements matter more than a dramatic one-time symptom story
presentation.typicalTimecourse: often recognized gradually through routine primary-care screening.

labs.name: basic metabolic panel and kidney function
labs.purpose: assess baseline organ effects and therapy safety
labs.expectedFinding: may be normal at diagnosis but helps risk-stratify care

diagnosticLogic: Confirm persistent elevation with repeated office and/or home readings rather than a single isolated measurement.
Evaluate for target-organ risk and secondary causes when the story suggests them.

differentials.whyConsidered: some patients have a reversible cause of elevated pressure
differentials.whyLessLikely: the routine ambulatory pattern without a specific trigger supports essential hypertension

escalation: titrate or combine medications to reach blood pressure targets safely
notes: Longitudinal prevention is the narrative payoff.

evidence.claimText: High blood pressure is frequently silent while still increasing the risk of stroke, heart disease, and kidney disease.
Hypertension diagnosis relies on persistent blood pressure elevation confirmed over time, not on symptoms alone.
Lifestyle changes and antihypertensive medication are combined to reduce long-term organ damage risk.

governanceNotes: Preferred baseline source for screening, risk, and diagnosis framing.
Kept for audit comparison only; superseded by the current baseline source.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Silent risk still deserves visual drama
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: Make the invisible vascular strain legible without pretending most patients feel a dramatic warning sign.

visualAnchors.label: Repeated blood-pressure checks beside organ-risk silhouettes
visualAnchors.whyItMatters: The page should look like longitudinal prevention rather than emergency collapse.

evidenceRelationships.notes: The disease can be silent, so diagnosis must come from repeated measurement rather than symptom certainty.

clinicalSummary.oneSentence: Chronic kidney disease is long-term loss of kidney structure or function that often begins silently and accumulates risk over time.
clinicalSummary.keyMechanism: Progressive nephron injury reduces filtration and raises albuminuria, cardiovascular burden, and medication-safety concerns.
clinicalSummary.patientExperienceSummary: Many patients feel normal early, so the detective work relies on labs, urine testing, and risk-factor context.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: Readers need a simple model for why filtration loss and albumin leak matter.

pathophysiology.event: ongoing nephron injury reduces kidney reserve
pathophysiology.mechanism: Filtration and albumin handling worsen as chronic damage accumulates.

physicalExamClues: chronic disease context matters more than a single dramatic symptom
presentation.typicalTimecourse: usually recognized through outpatient lab trends and urine testing.

labs.name: estimated GFR
labs.expectedFinding: reduced eGFR helps stage chronic kidney disease

labs.name: urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio
labs.expectedFinding: albuminuria supports kidney damage even when symptoms are absent

diagnosticLogic: Chronic kidney disease depends on persistent kidney damage or reduced function over time, not on a single abnormal lab alone.
Albuminuria and eGFR together help stage disease and refine risk.

differentials.whyLessLikely: the chronic trend and outpatient context support CKD

escalation: stage-based medication adjustment and nephrology referral when risk rises
notes: Primary care CKD work is largely longitudinal prevention and medication-safety management.

evidence.claimText: Chronic kidney disease often develops gradually and may be asymptomatic until kidney injury is established.
Estimated GFR and urine albumin testing are core tools for identifying and staging chronic kidney disease.
Management emphasizes risk-factor control, kidney-protective therapy when appropriate, and monitoring for progression.
evidence.sourceLabel: NIDDK chronic kidney disease care overview

governanceNotes: Preferred source for outpatient CKD screening and staging.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Lab trends are the clue trail
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: The detectives should earn CKD through urine and filtration clues instead of waiting for late symptoms.

visualAnchors.label: Longitudinal lab trend board with eGFR and albumin markers
visualAnchors.whyItMatters: CKD storytelling should feel slow-burn and data-driven.

evidenceRelationships.notes: Because early CKD can be silent, laboratory staging clues must carry the mystery.

clinicalSummary.oneSentence: Hypothyroidism is inadequate thyroid hormone effect, usually from primary thyroid gland failure, and often presents with gradual, nonspecific symptoms.
clinicalSummary.keyMechanism: Reduced thyroid hormone slows metabolic processes across many organ systems.
clinicalSummary.timeScale: typically develops gradually in outpatient care
clinicalSummary.patientExperienceSummary: Patients often describe fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, or weight change without a dramatic single clue.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: Readers need the pituitary-thyroid loop before TSH and free T4 clues make sense.

pathophysiology.mechanism: Reduced hormone signaling slows metabolism and produces broad systemic symptoms.

commonSigns: delayed reflex relaxation in some patients
physicalExamClues: symptoms are broad and nonspecific, so the story needs lab confirmation
presentation.typicalTimecourse: usually subtle enough that ambulatory longitudinal care matters.

labs.name: TSH
labs.purpose: screen for primary thyroid hormone insufficiency
labs.expectedFinding: elevated TSH suggests primary hypothyroidism when free T4 is low or low-normal

labs.name: free T4
labs.expectedFinding: low free T4 confirms overt hypothyroidism in the right context

diagnosticLogic: Symptoms are too nonspecific to stand alone, so the mystery must close with thyroid testing.
TSH and free T4 together distinguish overt hormone deficiency from milder or alternate patterns.

differentials.whyConsidered: fatigue and low energy are common in many conditions
differentials.whyLessLikely: thyroid testing and endocrine clues provide a firmer mechanism

firstLine: levothyroxine replacement when overt hypothyroidism is confirmed
escalation: dose titration based on symptom response and repeat TSH
notes: The payoff is usually steady outpatient normalization rather than an emergency intervention.

evidence.claimText: Hypothyroidism reflects inadequate thyroid hormone effect and often causes gradual fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, and dry skin.
Primary hypothyroidism is confirmed with thyroid function testing, especially TSH and free T4.
Levothyroxine replacement is the standard treatment for overt hypothyroidism, with follow-up lab monitoring for dose adjustment.

governanceNotes: Use for symptom and lab-confirmation framing.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Nonspecific symptoms still need a fair mechanism
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: The detectives should show why thyroid labs are necessary instead of pretending fatigue alone solves the mystery.

visualAnchors.label: Metabolic control board with TSH/free T4 clues
visualAnchors.whyItMatters: The visual logic should connect broad symptoms to endocrine measurement.

evidenceRelationships.notes: Because symptoms are nonspecific, thyroid testing is necessary to close the diagnostic loop.

clinicalSummary.oneSentence: Iron deficiency anemia is reduced hemoglobin production from inadequate iron availability, usually because intake, absorption, or blood loss cannot meet demand.
clinicalSummary.keyMechanism: Low iron limits hemoglobin synthesis, which lowers oxygen-carrying capacity and causes fatigue and exertional symptoms.
clinicalSummary.timeScale: often gradual, though the underlying source of blood loss may change urgency
clinicalSummary.patientExperienceSummary: Patients often describe fatigue, weakness, and reduced exercise tolerance long before they know why.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: The story needs a simple bridge from iron stores to reduced stamina and dyspnea.

pathophysiology.event: iron stores become insufficient for red cell production
pathophysiology.mechanism: Hemoglobin synthesis falls and red cells carry less oxygen effectively.

physicalExamClues: the clue trail should point to chronic oxygen-delivery strain and the source of iron loss
presentation.typicalTimecourse: often gradual, with discovery in primary care or outpatient workup.

labs.name: complete blood count
labs.expectedFinding: anemia with a pattern consistent with iron deficiency in context

labs.name: ferritin and iron studies
labs.expectedFinding: low ferritin strongly supports iron deficiency in the right setting

diagnosticLogic: Confirm iron deficiency biochemically, then look for the reason iron became inadequate.
The workup should not stop at replacement alone when bleeding or malabsorption is plausible.

differentials.whyConsidered: both can present with fatigue and low hemoglobin
differentials.whyLessLikely: iron studies and history help distinguish low iron stores from inflammatory restriction

firstLine: oral or other iron replacement when appropriate
evaluate and address the source of blood loss or malabsorption
escalation: consider higher-intensity replacement or specialist evaluation when oral therapy is insufficient or the cause is concerning
notes: Replacing iron without addressing the cause leaves the mystery unfinished.

evidence.claimText: Iron deficiency anemia results from insufficient iron availability for normal hemoglobin production.
Iron deficiency anemia is evaluated with a blood count plus iron studies such as ferritin to confirm low iron stores.
Treatment includes iron replacement and identification of the underlying reason iron loss or deficiency developed.

governanceNotes: Use for baseline ambulatory anemia mechanism, workup, and treatment.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Finish the cause hunt
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: The comic should not stop with low hemoglobin; it should show why identifying the iron-loss source matters.

visualAnchors.label: CBC and ferritin evidence board with a trail toward the bleeding source
visualAnchors.whyItMatters: This keeps the mystery tied to causal investigation rather than replacement alone.

evidenceRelationships.notes: Confirming iron deficiency is not the end of the workup; the cause still has to be found.

clinicalSummary.oneSentence: Migraine is a recurrent neurologic disorder that can cause disabling headache with sensory and autonomic symptoms.
clinicalSummary.keyMechanism: The disorder reflects episodic brain-network and trigeminovascular activation rather than a structural lesion found on routine imaging.
clinicalSummary.timeScale: recurrent attacks over months or years
clinicalSummary.patientExperienceSummary: Patients may experience throbbing head pain, nausea, light sensitivity, and sometimes aura, often with recognizable triggers.

physiologyPrerequisites.whyItMatters: Readers need a simple model for why migraine can produce both head pain and transient neurologic symptoms.

pathophysiology.mechanism: Attack physiology creates headache plus sensitivity to light, sound, movement, or nausea.

commonSigns: normal neurologic exam between attacks in uncomplicated migraine
physicalExamClues: the diagnosis is pattern-based and clinical unless red flags change the story
complications: stroke mimic confusion in some aura presentations
presentation.typicalTimecourse: recurrent attacks separated by symptom-free intervals.

diagnosticLogic: Migraine is diagnosed clinically from the attack pattern and associated features.
Imaging is reserved for red flags, not routine confirmation of a typical migraine story.

differentials.whyConsidered: dangerous causes can also present with headache
differentials.whyLessLikely: typical recurrent migraine pattern without red flags supports a primary headache disorder

firstLine: acute symptom treatment early in the attack
escalation: preventive therapy when attack burden or disability is frequent
notes: The story should distinguish acute attack treatment from prevention between attacks.

evidence.claimText: Migraine often causes recurrent headache with nausea and light or sound sensitivity, with some patients experiencing aura.
Migraine is primarily a clinical diagnosis, and routine imaging is not required for a typical pattern without red flags.
Migraine care separates acute treatment during attacks from preventive therapy when attack burden is frequent or disabling.

governanceNotes: Use for symptom pattern, diagnosis, and treatment framing.

clinicalTeachingPoints.title: Pattern recognition is the detective engine
clinicalTeachingPoints.teachingPoint: Migraine is usually solved by the pattern of recurrent attacks and associated symptoms, not by routine imaging spectacle.

visualAnchors.label: Attack calendar with aura symbols and light-sensitivity motif
visualAnchors.whyItMatters: The art should emphasize episodic recurrence and trigger pattern rather than a static lesion reveal.

evidenceRelationships.notes: The recognizable attack pattern explains why routine imaging is unnecessary in a typical case.
